subheading: Restyle a real room from a photo and keep its walls, windows, and dimensions intact using open, editable ComfyUI ControlNet workflows.
extendedDescription:
The first thing people ask of any ai interior design tool is whether it keeps the actual room or invents a fantasy one. Most consumer apps generate a brand-new picture loosely inspired by your photo, so the windows move, a wall disappears, and the proportions stop matching the space you are actually trying to renovate. The ComfyUI workflows on this page take a different route. They use ControlNet to read the depth and edges of your photo first, then redesign on top of that geometry, so the restyled room shares the same layout you started with.

That is the difference between a pretty render and a useful one. A depth ControlNet captures how far each surface sits from the camera, while a canny ControlNet traces the hard edges of walls, doorways, and furniture outlines. When you redesign a room from a photo with either map driving the generation, models like Flux, SD3.5, Qwen-Image, and Z-Image-Turbo repaint materials, lighting, and furnishings while the structure stays locked to the original. The result reads as the same room with a new look, not a different room entirely.

You control how literal that fidelity is. ControlNet exposes a strength setting: keep it high and the redesign hugs every edge of the existing space, which is what you want for remodel visualization where the client needs to recognize their own living room. Lower it and the model gets more creative latitude to reinterpret the layout, which suits early mood-boarding when you are still exploring directions. Being able to slide between faithful and exploratory on the same photo is something fixed one-click tools rarely allow.

The cluster covers the three jobs interior work actually involves. Whole-room restyling repaints an entire space into a chosen aesthetic from one reference photo. Layout-preserving redesign through depth or canny ControlNet, including the Qwen InstantX ControlNet workflow, holds the geometry steady while swapping the style. Targeted edits with Qwen-Image-Edit change only one thing, the flooring, a wall color, or a single piece of furniture, instead of regenerating everything, so you can iterate on details without losing the parts that already worked.

Virtual staging is one of the strongest uses here. Real-estate listings sell faster when rooms look furnished and current, and these workflows let you stage an empty or dated room straight from an MLS photo, then export a clean image for the listing. Because the layout is preserved, the staged photo still represents the real property rather than misleading buyers with a space that does not exist. You can run the same input through several interior design styles, modern, Scandinavian, minimalist, Japandi, mid-century, or industrial, to show one room multiple ways.

Everything runs from open, editable workflows, locally in ComfyUI or on Comfy Cloud with no install. There is no watermark stamped across the export and no cap on how many rooms you process. Treat the stack as an ai room planner you fully own: adjust the ControlNet strength, swap the underlying model to match your photo, and re-run any step until the redesign matches the space and the look you had in mind.

styles.title: Whole-room restyle
styles.description: Repaint an entire room into a new aesthetic from a single photo using Flux, Qwen-Image, or SD3.5 with ControlNet conditioning.

styles.title: Layout-preserving redesign
styles.description: Hold walls, windows, and dimensions in place with depth or canny ControlNet so the restyled space matches the real geometry.

styles.title: Targeted item edits
styles.description: Change only the flooring, a wall color, or one furniture piece with Qwen-Image-Edit instead of regenerating the whole room.

styles.title: Virtual staging
styles.description: Furnish an empty or dated room from an MLS photo for a real-estate listing while keeping the property's true layout.

styles.title: Multi-style exploration
styles.description: Run one photo through modern, Scandinavian, minimalist, Japandi, mid-century, and industrial looks to compare directions.

styles.title: Fidelity control
styles.description: Slide ControlNet strength between faithful remodel visualization and looser, creative reinterpretation of the same space.

howToUse: Pick the workflow that fits your goal: whole-room restyle, layout-preserving ControlNet redesign, or a targeted edit.
Open it on Comfy Cloud with no install, or load it in your local ComfyUI.
Upload a clear photo of the room you want to redesign, shot straight-on if possible.
Write a prompt describing the target style, then set the ControlNet strength for how closely to hold the layout.
Run the workflow and compare the redesign against your original to check the walls and windows still line up.
Refine the prompt or strength, re-run, and export the version you like at full resolution with no watermark.

faqItems.question: How does ai interior design keep my room's real layout?
faqItems.answer: These workflows run your photo through a ControlNet that reads its depth and edges before generating anything. The redesign is then painted on top of that geometry, so walls, windows, and proportions stay where they are. You are restyling the real room rather than inventing a new one.

faqItems.question: Can AI redesign a room from a photo without changing its dimensions?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Depth and canny ControlNet lock the structure of the photo while the model swaps materials, lighting, and furnishings. Keep the ControlNet strength high and the dimensions of the space stay faithful to your original shot.

faqItems.question: What interior design styles can these workflows generate?
faqItems.answer: You drive the style through the text prompt, so modern, Scandinavian, minimalist, Japandi, mid-century, and industrial all work. Running one photo through several prompts is a fast way to compare directions. The layout stays constant while only the aesthetic changes.

faqItems.question: Can I run these ai interior design workflows myself without limits?
faqItems.answer: These ComfyUI workflows are open and editable. You can run them locally or on Comfy Cloud with no install, and there is no cap on rooms or watermark on the export. That makes them practical as an ai room planner you process many rooms through.

faqItems.question: Can I change only the flooring or one piece of furniture?
faqItems.answer: Yes, with the Qwen-Image-Edit workflow. It applies a targeted edit to a single element, so you can swap the floor or recolor a wall without regenerating the rest of the room. The parts you already like stay untouched.

faqItems.question: Does this work for virtual staging real-estate listings?
faqItems.answer: It does. You can furnish an empty or dated room from an MLS photo and export a clean image for the listing. Because the layout is preserved, the staged photo still represents the real property instead of a fictional space.

faqItems.question: How do I control how creative the redesign gets?
faqItems.answer: ControlNet exposes a strength value that sets how tightly the result follows your photo. High strength gives faithful remodel visualization that clients will recognize, while lower strength lets the model reinterpret the room for early mood-boarding. You can re-run the same photo at different strengths to find the balance.

faqItems.question: Why do the redesigns look like the same room rather than a new one?
faqItems.answer: Because the generation is conditioned on the actual depth and edges of your photo, not just a loose text description. The model repaints surfaces and decor on top of fixed geometry, so the output shares your room's structure. This is what makes the result useful for an actual renovation.
